section.title: Restraint and Relationships
section.sectionRationale: This chapter sits inside the first great collection, the Proverbs of Solomon, 10:1 to 22:16, a long run of independent sentence proverbs. Chapter 17 keeps circling one seam: the restrained life, quiet, truthful, unretaliating, is stronger and safer than a life that grasps for advantage through strife, bribery, or a loose tongue.
section.thesis: Peace with a little is better than plenty won through quarreling, because the LORD tests every heart, rewards a guarded tongue and a loyal friend, and calls His people away from strife, favoritism, and retaliation toward quiet self-control.
section.frameNote: Seven clusters: peace over plenty and the LORD who tests hearts; words that heal or wound; the high cost of strife and crooked judgment; wisdom's worth and a friend who loves at all times; the heart under pressure; favoritism, bribery, and a grieving father; the wisdom of a closed mouth.

verses.text: Better a dry morsel in quietness than a house full of feasting with strife.
A wise servant will rule over a disgraceful son and share his inheritance as one of the brothers.
A crucible for silver and a furnace for gold, but the LORD is the {{bochan|tester}} of hearts.
A wicked man listens to evil lips; a liar gives ear to a destructive tongue.
He who mocks the poor insults their Maker; whoever gloats over calamity will not go unpunished.
Grandchildren are the crown of the aged, and the glory of a son is his father.
Eloquent words are unfit for a fool; how much worse are lying lips to a ruler!
A bribe is a charm to its giver; wherever he turns, he succeeds.
Whoever conceals an offense promotes love, but he who brings it up separates friends.
A rebuke cuts into a man of discernment deeper than a hundred lashes cut into a fool.
An evil man seeks only rebellion; a cruel messenger will be sent against him.
It is better to meet a bear robbed of her cubs than a {{kesil|fool}} in his folly.
If anyone returns evil for good, evil will never leave his house.
To start a quarrel is to release a flood; so abandon the dispute before it breaks out.
Acquitting the guilty and condemning the righteous, both are detestable to the LORD.

units.label: Wisdom's worth and a loyal friend
units.note: a friend loves at all times

verses.text: Why should the fool have money in his hand with no intention of buying wisdom?
A {{rea|friend}} loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity.
A man lacking judgment strikes hands in pledge and puts up security for his neighbor.
He who loves transgression loves strife; he who builds his gate high invites destruction.

units.label: The heart under pressure
units.note: grief, deceit, and a joyful heart

verses.text: The one with a perverse heart finds no good, and he whose tongue is deceitful falls into trouble.
A man fathers a fool to his own grief; the father of a fool has no joy.
A joyful heart is good medicine, but a broken spirit dries up the bones.
A wicked man takes a covert bribe to subvert the course of justice.
Wisdom is the focus of the discerning, but the eyes of a fool wander to the ends of the earth.
A foolish son brings grief to his father and bitterness to her who bore him.
It is surely not good to punish the innocent or to flog a noble for his honesty.

units.label: The wisdom of a closed mouth

verses.text: A man of knowledge restrains his words, and a man of understanding maintains a calm spirit.
Even a fool is considered wise if he keeps silent, and discerning when he holds his tongue.

bochan.gloss: Tester, prover, one who assays metal for purity. Verse 3 sets the smith's crucible and furnace beside the LORD, who tests hearts the way a refiner tests silver and gold, seeing past outward behavior to what a person truly loves.

kesil.gloss: Fool, the thickheaded, stubborn person who resists correction and lives by impulse rather than wisdom. Proverbs judges him more dangerous to confront than a wild animal robbed of her cubs, since a fool's folly is unpredictable and unreasoning.

rea.gloss: Friend, companion, neighbor, one bound to another by covenant loyalty rather than mere convenience. Verse 17 says this friend loves 'at all times,' constant through every season, a love the New Testament finds perfected in Christ.

basic.context: This chapter belongs to the long first collection of Solomon's proverbs, and it keeps circling one idea: restraint is strength. A dry morsel eaten in peace beats a feast torn apart by strife, a rebuke received humbly does more good than a hundred lashes on a fool, and a friend who loves at all times is worth more than money spent chasing wisdom you never intend to use.
basic.hermeneutics: These are general observations about wise living, not legal guarantees; not every dispute can be avoided and not every fool learns from rebuke. The proverbs describe how life usually rewards restraint and self-control and usually punishes strife, bribery, and retaliation, and they call the reader to choose the wise path even when results are not automatic.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD is called a bochan, a tester or refiner, who assays the heart the way a smith assays metal, verse 3. A kesil, a stubborn fool, is judged more dangerous to meet than a robbed bear, verse 12. And a rea, a covenant friend, loves at all times, verse 17, a love that anticipates the New Testament's picture of Christ as the friend who never lets go.
basic.typology: The friend who loves at all times and the brother born for adversity, verse 17, foreshadow Christ, the friend of sinners who lays down His life for those He loves. The LORD who tests hearts, verse 3, anticipates the final testing and vindication that comes only through Him who knew no sin.
basic.christ: Verse 17's friend who loves at all times finds its fullest answer in Jesus, who called His disciples friends and laid down His life for them, John 15:13 to 15. Where Proverbs 17 warns that returning evil for good never leaves a house, verse 13, Christ absorbed evil for good on the cross and broke that curse for all who trust Him.
basic.god: God is the tester of hearts, verse 3, who sees past behavior to motive, and He detests both acquitting the guilty and condemning the righteous, verse 15, because He is just. Yet He is also the Maker whom the poor bear His image, verse 5, so mocking them is mocking Him; His justice and His compassion are inseparable.
basic.crossReferences: John 15:13 to 15 shows Christ as the friend who loves at all times and lays down His life. Romans 12:17 to 19 echoes the warning against repaying evil for evil. James 3 speaks to the restrained tongue. 1 Corinthians 4:5 points to the God who tests hearts and will bring hidden things to light.
basic.oneStory: Proverbs 17 calls God's people to restraint, quiet contentment, loyal friendship, and a guarded tongue, because the LORD who tests every heart is not fooled by outward success won through strife. The whole story of Scripture answers this call in Jesus, the friend who loves at all times and whose peace is better than any feast bought with strife.

context.literary: Chapter 17 is a chain of independent, mostly unconnected sentence proverbs within the first Solomonic collection, 10:1 to 22:16. Its unity comes from recurring themes rather than a single argument: strife versus peace, folly versus discernment, and the guarded versus the loose tongue.
context.historical: In an honor and shame culture where feasting, inheritance, and public rebuke carried heavy social weight, these proverbs trained the young to value quiet integrity over the noisy pursuit of advantage, warning especially against bribery and favoritism in the courts of a small agrarian society where everyone knew everyone.
context.canonical: This chapter's warnings against returning evil for evil and its praise of the friend who loves at all times anticipate New Testament ethics of non-retaliation and sacrificial friendship, most fully realized in Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To train the reader in the discipline of restraint, showing through short, memorable sayings that a guarded tongue, a loyal friend, and refusal to retaliate are wiser and safer than strife, bribery, or grasping for advantage.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Most of chapter 17 prescribes wise conduct directly, restrain your words, love at all times, do not take bribes, but several verses simply describe how life tends to run, a fool's folly is dangerous, grief follows a foolish son. Readers should not turn these general observations into ironclad promises, since Proverbs itself elsewhere shows exceptions.

debates.question: Does verse 21, 'the father of a fool has no joy,' blame parents for how children turn out?

views.label: Yes, it is mainly about parental failure
views.case: Since Proverbs elsewhere commands parents to train children, verse 21 could be read as exposing bad parenting.

views.label: No, it describes grief without assigning blame
views.case: Wisdom literature consistently allows for children who reject good training, so the verse names painful reality rather than pronouncing verdict on parents; the emphasis falls on the child's foolish choices, not a formula for parental guilt.

debates.bearing: Read as description rather than blame, the proverb comforts grieving parents rather than condemning them, and keeps responsibility where Scripture places it, on the choices of the child as well as the parent.

study.originalLanguages: Three words anchor the chapter. The LORD is a bochan, a tester who assays hearts like metal, verse 3. A kesil, a hardened fool, is more perilous to cross than a robbed bear, verse 12. And a rea, a covenant friend, loves at all times, verse 17, the Hebrew idiom underlining constancy rather than mere sentiment.

typology.type: The friend who loves at all times, 17:17
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who calls His disciples friends and lays down His life for them, John 15:13-15.
The final testing and judgment entrusted to Christ, who alone knows what is in a person, John 2:24-25.

study.christ: Proverbs 17 prizes a friend who loves at all times and a brother born for adversity, verse 17, language the New Testament fills out in Jesus, who is friend to sinners and lays down His life for those He loves, John 15:13 to 15. The chapter also warns that repaying evil for good never leaves a house, verse 13, yet at the cross Christ absorbed the world's evil and returned it with good, breaking that pattern for everyone united to Him by faith. He is both the tested and tried Son who proved flawless, and the refuge for hearts the LORD is still testing.
study.god: God tests hearts the way a refiner tests silver, verse 3, looking past behavior to motive, and He is equally offended by acquitting the guilty and condemning the righteous, verse 15, since both pervert the justice that flows from His own character. He is also the Maker of the poor, verse 5, so contempt for the poor is contempt for Him; His holiness and His compassion for the vulnerable are one and the same commitment to what is right.

crossReferences.note: Christ names His disciples friends and lays down His life for them, fulfilling the friend who loves at all times.
Do not repay anyone evil for evil, echoing the warning of Proverbs 17:13.
The call to restrain speech and tame the tongue develops Proverbs 17:27-28.
The Lord will bring to light what is hidden and disclose the motives of hearts, echoing the LORD as tester in 17:3.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 17 teaches restraint as the path of the wise: peace over plenty, a guarded tongue, a loyal friend, no repayment of evil for evil. This whole way of life is embodied and fulfilled in Jesus, the friend who loves at all times, who bore evil with good at the cross and now tests and refines the hearts of His people until they share His likeness.

quiz.q: According to verse 1, what is better than a house full of feasting?

options.text: A dry morsel eaten in quietness, without strife.
options.note: Peace with little outweighs plenty torn apart by conflict.

options.text: A feast shared among many guests.
options.note: Feasting itself is not condemned; the issue is strife accompanying it.

options.text: A feast eaten alone in silence.
options.note: The verse contrasts quietness with strife, not company with solitude.

options.text: There is no comparison offered in the verse.
options.note: The verse is explicitly a 'better than' comparison.

quiz.q: What does verse 3 say the LORD does to hearts?

options.text: He tests them, as a crucible tests silver and a furnace tests gold.
options.note: The LORD is pictured as the tester or refiner of the heart's true metal.

options.text: He ignores the heart and judges only outward actions.
options.note: The verse specifically names the heart as what is tested, not merely behavior.

options.text: He hardens every heart without exception.
options.note: This misreads testing as hardening; the verse describes discernment, not fixed hardening.

options.text: He rewards hearts that appear silver or gold in color.
options.note: The metaphor of silver and gold is present but points to purity and value, not literal appearance.

quiz.q: What does verse 17 say about a true friend?

options.text: A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity.
options.note: Constancy defines real friendship, especially in hardship.

options.text: A friend loves only when it is convenient.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's point about constancy.

options.text: A friend is mainly useful for business dealings.
options.note: The proverb speaks of love and adversity, not commercial usefulness.

options.text: A friend loves most of the time, but not during hardship.
options.note: Close, but it reverses the emphasis; adversity is precisely when this love proves real.

quiz.q: How does verse 13 describe the result of returning evil for good?

options.text: Evil will never leave that person's house.
options.note: Repaying good with evil brings lasting trouble on the offender.

options.text: It brings temporary trouble that quickly passes.
options.note: The verse's language is stronger and more lasting than 'temporary.'

options.text: It brings no consequence at all.
options.note: The verse explicitly warns of consequence, not immunity.

options.text: It only affects the person who was wronged.
options.note: The verse locates the consequence in the wrongdoer's own house, not merely the victim's.

quiz.q: What does the chapter commend about speech in verses 27 to 28?

options.text: Restraint: a knowledgeable person holds back words and even a fool seems wise if silent.
options.note: Verses 27 and 28 both praise restrained, calm speech.

options.text: Eloquence: speaking often and impressively.
options.note: The chapter elsewhere criticizes eloquent words wasted on fools, verse 7; the emphasis here is restraint, not eloquence.

options.text: Silence in every circumstance, never speaking.
options.note: Close, but the proverbs commend restraint and calm spirit, not an absolute vow of silence.

options.text: Loud confidence in speech.
options.note: This is the opposite of the calm, restrained spirit these verses commend.

questions: Verse 1 says a dry morsel in peace beats a feast full of strife. Where in your life are you tempted to chase more at the cost of peace?
The LORD is described as the tester of hearts, verse 3. What would He find if He tested the true motives behind your recent words or decisions?
A friend loves at all times, verse 17. Who has been that kind of friend to you, and who might need you to be that kind of friend to them this week?
Verse 13 warns that repaying evil for good brings lasting trouble. Is there a grudge or a tit for tat pattern you are holding onto that this proverb calls you to release?
Verses 27 and 28 praise restrained speech. What would change in your closest relationship if you held your tongue for one extra breath before responding?

translationNotes.note: Bochan, rendered 'tester,' pictures a metallurgist assaying ore; some translations render it 'prover' or 'refiner,' each capturing the same image of testing for purity.
'To start a quarrel is to release a flood' translates an image of breaching a dam; once released, a quarrel is very hard to contain again.
'A joyful heart is good medicine' can also be rendered 'does good like a medicine,' underscoring that emotional health has bodily effect, an idea the Hebrew ties closely to the body's 'bones.'

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 17. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
